sentences.text: Yes.
I think gold, diamonds, like I said, in the clothes, in the shoes, even the woman's body they were hiding something, in the janitorial places.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And our-- our soup, because we were not eating soup anymore, because we had bread and butter with those-- with those things, our soup, they were-- we were giving to another camp for the children.
Our soup every day was going instead to come to us, we're going to help them for more-- with more soup.
No way, no way.
Sleeping, eating, and working, right over there, everything right in there, no way to get in contact.
In the beginning, we were going to a camp.
We had a special barracks for it, another barracks with us in the beginning.
But after there, we was limping right to the crematoriums.
No.
Even at the barracks we were over there, we couldn't contact them.
No.
No, no.
Was all with the guards, with SS, SS men guard.
We didn't have a chance, only one time.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They brought us from the camps about 100, 150, 200.
They brought it to us.
And they were waiting.
They were waiting downstairs to get killed with a-- with a gun because it was a few of [? them. ?] Over there, I saw my first cousin, my father's first cousin, Leo Venezia called.
He was so strong guy.
When he came in over, there he was like that.
And another guy, called him Baru Venezia, which was a big-- in Salonika, he was a big businessman from-- for men's clothes, one of the biggest in Salonika and [INAUDIBLE].
I saw them, told them which.
They were familiar.
I mean, one was my second cousin.
The only thing I told them is, are you hungry?
He said, please, please.
You know where you're going?
You know, all of you over here, you're going-- they're going to kill you right now.
I told them just like that.
Who cares, he said.
Have you something to eat?
And I went-- I went in my place over there in my bed.
Under the bed I had tin meat, was about, I can tell you, pound of tin meat.
I open it.
I didn't even see it when he ate it.
He swallow it.
He didn't eat it.
And he didn't care if he were going to get killed or not.
So for those two guys, after they got killed, we cleaned the oven in the crematorium.
We got their bones.
They put them in a kind of a cans.
We open a little drain somewhere over there in the place, and we prayed for them.
We said the Ashkaba.
That's what we could do.
Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with us, they were working three brothers from Salonika.
I don't remember the older one name.
But I remember the two names.
Once, his name was Albert Jahom, Jahom, with a J.
Did you read about it in the diary maybe?
And the other one was Raul Jahom, the younger one.
The older one I don't remember the name.
Many, every-- every time for lunch time, two, three of us, they would go into the kitchen to get the soup.
But this not was a reason to go to the soup because the soup was going for the children, because we had some gold with us or some diamonds or something.
We did want to give it to the kapo at the time.
The kapo was giving it to the-- so we were going-- we were thinking to going right away into the kitchen to get some margarine and some cinnamon, [INAUDIBLE].
And this Raul Jahom, a gorgeous guy, so handsome, so strong, so young, he went over there some gold in the kitchen.
And I don't know how he got call from the SS man.
And he brought it to us over there.
We were [INAUDIBLE] at the crematoriums.
He told him to take off his clothes.
And he laid down.
And that German shoot him right on the belly, not to kill him.
And, of course, this [INAUDIBLE], he was screaming from the pains.
He was begging to the SS man to kill him.
And the SS man just shot him another-- another time right on the belly.
He didn't want to kill him.
It took three shots, because Raul was begging him to kill him.
Three shots, and Raul to get killed.
Was another guy, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know why he did that, that guy, that SS man.
He put on some gasoline, and he put a match.
And he told him to run right down the wires over there to escape from there.
When he was along the wires, he shot him.
He tried to escape, and I shot him.
That was the reason.
Who was that?
Number 182 something.
Do you remember that man's name?
No.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Why?
They got about 200, 300 Greek people and put them to work at the crematoriums.
The people they had before, 1, 2, 3, and 4 crematoriums was enough, because was at least 100 people working in each crematorium.
Why they took another 400 people, maybe, Greeks, 300 people, I don't know.
I don't remember how many or why they took. 100, 300, 400 I don't know.
That was the reason, because they put us to open some trenches, big trenches in the same lot of the crematoriums, was about two or three big ones.
I can tell you maybe 100 feet, maybe more, with 50, 60 feet large, because they were waiting all the Jewish from Hungary.
They took some Jewish from Hungary before, but I don't know why those Jewish, they couldn't take her away, was political?
Think-- I don't know.
And was all the-- those rich Jewish from Theresienstadt.
We know because they were Jewish, because we found a lot of gold, and diamonds, et cetera, et cetera from those people.
And because [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in a few words, they killed them about 30,000, I can tell you, in about two weeks, three weeks, 30,000 of the Hungarian people.
And those strangers, what they did, they put some wood in the bottom and some gasoline.
And all this wood and the gasoline was burning all the time.
And from the gas chamber, working 24 hours a day, taking all those dead bodies and threw them right in those trenches.
And they were burning this time because the ovens was not enough to burn so many people in so long-- in so little time.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And it happened to work me night time with that kind of cold, et cetera, et cetera.
It's so many things to say, but I cannot say it anymore.
I don't know what-- how it happened over there, three beautiful Hungarian girls, young, beautiful Hungarian girls, they didn't want to go into the gas chamber.
They-- I don't know what happened.
There was over there an officer, SS.
They didn't force them to take them into the chamber.
They took them out right on those-- they were-- they were burning the other ones of the-- those trenches.
He took his revolver and he shot the girls in front of us.
If I remember was that the bullet went through the first and the second.
The third one, I think, fainted.
And they went down, the three of them together.
One went through my-- the trench was there.
They went just we took one with two hands and threw them in there to get burned.
The third one, when we threw them in there, we heard some screaming.
She was not dead, because the bullet passed only the two girls.
The third girl didn't pass.
So if they wanted to kill us, why didn't killed us?
Why they make us suffer first and then to kill us with hungry, with hunger, or with-- or bitterness till death?
Why did they do that?
We are not human beings, just because we are Jewish?
Somebody's a-- it's a democratic, or a communist, or all those-- those group of thinking.
But everybody are human beings.
Just because we were Jewish, they were doing that?
What that means Jewish?
The Jewish people of God?
Why God don't choose somebody else not to be-- the [INAUDIBLE] the-- and leave us alone not to suffer anymore.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So 24 hours working in those trenches for 30,000 people.
Can you imagine how much gold they got?
Can you imagine my-- how many shoes, how many clothes, how many everything else?
Even with hair, the hair of the women, they got everything from us, those bunch of thieves.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I can-- what can I say now?
The new generation, what they know about it?
They don't know nothing about it.
But what animals, all of them.
Now, after that, we were talking about the revolution.
Before we get to that, let me just ask you, when the Hungarians were brought in to Birkenau, do you remember when this was, what time of year?
No, at the end, before we got liberated, maybe about-- maybe in May was again.
I don't remember exactly.
Don't blame me, Carol, because I cannot remember everything.
It is impossible.
And I get so emotional when I say something.
Now, I remember 50 years ago what happened to us, something impossible, something that human brains cannot imagine will happen.
No matter how the books writing or the pictures from the troops they're doing, is nothing like when you wearing their suffering every day.
That's impossible for a human being-- I say that again-- to imagine what really we were suffering.
Mhm.
OK.

[CRYING] In the beginning, all the ashes from the oven, we were taking out, put 'em in a truck, and a couple Germans in the truck with three, four of us going to the nearest river was over there, the river called Vistula, and threw in the ashes over there.
But, of course, the ashes are very light.
And all those ashes on the river was floating on top.
And I don't know that river whether it was going to Poland or to Russian, I don't know.
One day, they stop getting all those-- those ashes, because wasn't just ashes, it was bones, whole bones, because it don't becoming ashes.
These whole bones, the bones, the big bones and so on, they don't become ashes.
They were still very hard.
One day, they took us to cut some trees, some trunks of the trees.
And we made kind of a tool to smash the-- the ashes.
And they made us build a kind of a cement on the floor outside of the crematorium, outside, I mean, in the same place, of cement.
And all the ashes, all those bones burning, burned bones, they were coming to this place.
And about 10, 20, 30 of us with this kind of a tool of the trunks of the trees, they were smashing the-- those-- with those bones and making ashes.
Right.
All this time, yes.
As a matter of fact, Dario and me, we were working crematorium three.
And my brother was in crematorium two, because my brother knew how to speak Poland, just-- just like that in German.
And they knew about the revolution we were talking about.
And my brother wanted to have me and Dario and his brother in crematorium two, not to work in crematorium three, to be together over there.
So one day, he was very acquainted with a kapo, my brother.
One day, they took Dario and me.
They knew we were singing, Dario and me, together and to have some-- some fun with us in the crematorium two, because my brother told them that they sing and so, and so, and so, and so.
They took us to crematorium two-- two.
And we were working together over there, together.
And I remember it over there was 18 persons.
They were prisoner of [INAUDIBLE], or war.
I don't know how they brought him over there.
The only name I remember of those guys, because he got killed from the German, and I tell you how.
This morning, I remember his name.
Before you come, I remember his name.
I don't remember now.
[LAUGHS] Well.
Italian songs.
No, we were singing from Salonika with Dario.
We know all the Italian songs, especially for [?
Firenze. ?] [?
Firenze ?] [INAUDIBLE].
It helped because the kapo was given us a piece of bread sometime, something, or schnapps calling.
Sure, and then we had some-- what's a tempo, what they call him, some fun.
To pass the time, yeah, when we were not working.
Not when we were working, no.
When we were not working.
Only when we were going to bed.
I remember all the time, especially when we were in quarantine, we were too hungry, talking about the food our mothers were doing.
And the saliva was coming, you know?
That's-- that's The only talking we had with everybody about food, all the time.
No, not even 1%, not even 1%.
No hope, no hope at all.
Why?
Especially from the Sonderkommando, because they were-- they were killing-- they were killing if Sonderkommando was so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody knew about it.
I mean, to getting caught at what we were doing over there was sort of-- oh, what do they call them?
Not mysterious, so--
Isolated that nobody could believe what was going on over there.
Nobody, even the-- the people that were right in the camp.
They didn't know.
Even before the-- the German to-- to take us, we didn't know that they were killing.
Nobody knew it.
How long would we last?
The work over there?
Nothing over there under the Sonderkommando, because they were not in contact with nobody else, and they knew the job over there.
When we went over there, the Greeks, to work for the Hungarian, to burn the Hungarian bodies, were all Polands.
And were a kapo-- kapo with a German kapo, a criminal, like I told you, Karlo, name, which at the end we killed them.
And after we burned the 30,000 Hungarian people, then they start to get some of us and kill them.
Because was no-- no-- no work anymore.
And the Russian was coming.
Was no work anymore more to [INAUDIBLE].
Was no more-- no more transports.
The last ones was the Hungarians, 30,000.
And after that, they got some other Hungarians, which I found them in [GERMAN] with the hair again and with some belongings they had.
They didn't have the time to kill them.
When there was no more work, that when-- there is where we start-- they were-- they start.
They thought, maybe, was the revolution.
There-- there-- then-- then it started like this.
If you want to hear about it, I can tell you right away for the revolution.
We're coming near the revolution now.
About the revolt?
I'll tell you right now.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the-- in the crematoriums, was working with us a Greek guy, a very short Greek guy, which he was an officer in the Greek army cavalry.
Joseph was his name.
I don't know the second name.
I don't remember.
Maybe Dario get it, told you.
That was a military type.
And the way it was, the crematoriums, one, two, three, and four, was in each one of them was separated, except the Kanada Kommando, Kanada Kommando.
It was streets, which they were coming from Auschwitz form the end of the Birkenau.
From the inside of Birkenau, they were coming to the west, to us, walking in those streets.
I can make you a design if you want to.
Rifles and--
Machine guns to go and get the post.
Was two or three, I don't remember exactly.
And each way they were coming, every night, every night, every night, every night.
They were changing guards.
So this Baru, Joseph, I mean, not Baru.
Baru, his father, you know, which we killed him.
They killed him over there, and like I told you with my cousin.
That he was his father, you know?
So they said with friends, talking about it, we're going to get killed anyway.
He said, what about if we make something, maybe somebody is going to be liberated.
And he gave us all his points, how and when.
In the meantime, we heard about it that partisans, Polands, they were in contact with us, with somebody else in the camp.
I don't remember exactly.
But actually, with a kapo of us, a Poland kapo.
They were in contact with us.
So how it was, this crazy what I made, because once I had, he had brains.

So in the crematorium [INAUDIBLE], going like this, two, three, and four, coming this, we got two guards in each crematorium, which we are 200 people, 300 people working. 200 at least, 200 people working each crematorium at the time.
We can kill them, those two guards.
Then when they pass by, get his rifles, his arms.
And going out when they pass by, just jump on them.
There are two or three of them.
We are so many.
Get their clothing.
Get the machine guns and so on.
In the same time, in the four crematoriums.
So we are [INAUDIBLE], eight Germans.
We can have eight machine guns.
And so that was a good idea of him.
And all the Greeks agree.
Then from them, he said, we're going route right into the Birkenau on the barracks.
Were going to make a mess over there.
And from there, who knows?
God knows, we're going to Auschwitz.
We're going to get killed.
We know that.
But over here, we're working for them.
We're going to get killed anyway.
So the Greek people all agree.
The Poland people, they were so strong and so big, they were afraid somehow.
They were not just-- to believe in it like the Greek people.
The Greek people were more-- more courageous.
In the meantime, this Joseph was talking all the time with a kapo we had, with a-- a short guy of kapo.
And the kapo was agree with him, because it was two, three years working over there with the kapo.
We're going to stop now and change the tape.
Hm.
